title: Coin Toss Game (n+1 vs n)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Gambler A has (n+1) fair coins. Gambler B has n fair coins. Both flip ALL of their coins. We compare the number of heads each got.
rules: All coins are fair (heads with probability 1/2, independent).
A 'wins' if A has STRICTLY more heads than B.
If A and B tie, A does not win.
setup.question: What is the probability that A has strictly more heads than B?

learningObjectives: Reduce A's (n+1)-coin total to A's n-coin total plus one extra coin.
Use symmetry on the n-coin sub-game to show P(A's n > B's n) = P(A's n < B's n).
Combine the symmetric probabilities with the extra coin's 1/2 to get 1/2.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you compute P(A wins) without summing over every binomial outcome?

options.text: Symmetry: temporarily set aside one of A's coins. Compare A's remaining n vs B's n -- by symmetry the probabilities of tie / A-more / A-less match those of B's hand.
Sum P(A has k heads) * P(B has < k heads) over k.

stages.explanation: Symmetry beats brute force. The (n+1) vs n setup is asymmetric -- but if you peel off the extra coin, the underlying n-vs-n game IS symmetric.

stages.label: Identify the three sub-events
stages.prompt: Set aside one of A's coins. The remaining game (A's n coins vs B's n coins) has three mutually exclusive outcomes. Pick all three.

options.text: E1: A's n has MORE heads than B's n
E2: A's n TIES B's n
E3: A's n has FEWER heads than B's n
E4: the extra coin lands tails

stages.explanation: Three exhaustive cases for the n-vs-n sub-game. The extra coin is a separate (independent) event.

stages.label: Use symmetry on the n-vs-n game
stages.prompt: By symmetry of the n-vs-n sub-game (both players have n coins), what's the relationship between P(E1) and P(E3)?

options.text: P(E1) = P(E3) = x.  Let P(E2) = y.  Then 2x + y = 1.
P(E1) > P(E3) since A has the extra coin.

stages.explanation: A's n coins and B's n coins are statistically identical, so 'A more' and 'A fewer' are equally likely. They sum with the tie to 1.

stages.label: Add back A's extra coin
stages.prompt: Now restore A's extra coin (heads with prob 1/2). When does the extra coin matter?

options.text: In E1: A already wins regardless of the extra coin.  In E3: A loses regardless.  In E2: A wins iff the extra coin is heads (prob 1/2).  Total: P(A wins) = x + (1/2)*y.
The extra coin always tilts toward A.

stages.explanation: The extra coin breaks the tie in A's favor 50% of the time. In E1 and E3 the extra coin can't change the outcome. So total P(A wins) = P(E1) + (1/2)*P(E2) = x + y/2.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: From 2x + y = 1 and P(A wins) = x + y/2, evaluate P(A wins).

options.text: Greater than 1/2 because A has more coins.

stages.explanation: Substituting y = 1 - 2x: P(A wins) = x + (1 - 2x)/2 = x + 1/2 - x = 1/2. Independent of n. The extra coin exactly compensates for the tie-breaking asymmetry.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What if A had n+2 coins instead of n+1? Compute P(A wins).
Verify the answer for n = 0 (A has 1 coin, B has 0): P(A wins) = P(A flips heads) = 1/2 ✓.
Why is the answer EXACTLY 1/2 and not slightly different?
